text: The surgeon performs the procedure using an anterior transperitoneal approach, incising the peritoneum at the intersection of the medial umbilical ligament and the vas deferens. The paravesical space is exposed until the endopelvic fascia is visible. The urachus is coagulated and cut, followed by a longitudinal cystostomy at the prostate-vesicle junction. A stitch is placed in the middle lobe for traction. The bilateral ureteric orifices are identified to prevent injury. Sharp and blunt dissection develops the plane between the prostatic adenoma and pseudocapsule, starting at the six o'clock position on the right lobe, then the left lobe. The prostatic urethra is transected at the apex, and the prostatic adenoma is fully dissected. Hemostasis is achieved, and the prostatic fossa is retrigonized with a single-layer running suture from the urethra to the bladder neck. The cystotomy is closed with a double-layer running suture, and a three-way catheter is placed for continuous irrigation.

The surgeon performs the procedure using a posterior approach, starting with a posterior midline cystostomy. The bladder is retracted using three exovulporal sutures, and the bilateral ureteric orifices are identified to prevent injury. The bladder neck is incised at the six o'clock position to expose the prostatic adenoma. Sharp and blunt dissection is used to develop the plane between the prostatic adenoma and the pseudocapsule circumferentially on both sides. After complete dissection, the prostatic fossa is inspected for hemostasis, and single stitches are placed to prevent bleeding. A three-way catheter with continuous irrigation is inserted, and the cystostomy incision is closed with single stitches.
